revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:伊豆山権現]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Statue of Izusan Gongen at Hannya-in front.jpg|venerated_in={{ill|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}}}}
[[File:Statue of Izusan Gongen at Hannya-in front.jpg|thumb|Wooden Statue of Izusan Gongen (held at Sōtōzan Hannya-in, Atami City, Shizuoka Prefecture, {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}). This statue was enshrined at Izusan Shrine until the [[:en:Shinbutsu Bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and was later moved to Hannya-in.]]
'''Izusan Gongen''' (伊豆山権現) is a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} deity originating from the {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}} and {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} practices of {{ill|Izusan|ja|伊豆山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izusan|1=Izusan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11380565}} (Sōtōzan). It is identified with {{ill|Senju Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|qid=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}}, and {{ill|Cintāmaṇicakra|en|Cintāmaṇicakra|qid=Q11446712|lt=Nyoirin Kannon|1=Cintāmaṇicakra}} as its {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|qid=Q3080781|lt=Honji suijaku|1=Honji suijaku}} forms. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|qid=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}, Izusan Gongen was enshrined at Izusan Gongen Shrine (now {{ill|Izusan Shrine|en|Izusan Shrine|qid=Q611460|lt=Izusan Shrine|1=Izusan Shrine}}) and Sōtōzan Hannya-in. It was also known as '''Izusan Sansho Gongen''' (伊豆山三所権現) and '''Sōtō Gongen''' (走湯権現).
The 1896 edition of *Dai Nihon Jinmei Jisho* states that it enshrines {{ill|Hōmusubi-no-kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōmusubi-no-kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}}, also known as {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}}.{{Sfn|Keizai Zasshisha|1896|p=241}}
== History ==
=== Establishment === {{ill|Izusan|ja|伊豆山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izusan|1=Izusan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11380565}}, located in present-day {{ill|Atami|en|Atami|qid=Q653364|lt=Atami, Shizuoka|1=Atami}}, was historically referred to as *Sōtōzan* (走湯山), meaning "running hot springs mountain," due to its geothermal activity along the coast. This made it an early site of {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}}.<ref name="jinja">[https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kanko/kankoshisetsu/1001839/1001864/1001869.html About Izusan Shrine] - Atami City, accessed February 8, 2020.</ref>
According to the shrine tradition of Izusan Shrine, its establishment dates back to the 5th or 4th century BCE during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kōshō|en|Emperor Kōshō|qid=Q312841|lt=Emperor Kōshō|1=Emperor Kōshō}}. The divine spirit (*Goshinrei*) of Izusan Gongen was initially enshrined at *Higane-san* (日金山, also known as Kurachi-yama, near present-day Atami Pass) before being relocated to *Hongū-san* (本宮山, near the present *Hongūsha* of Izusan Shrine).<ref name="jinja" />
Another tradition states that during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, a round mirror appeared at the summit of Higane-san. A hermit named Matsuba Sennin enshrined it, marking the origin of Sōtō Gongen. Later, three additional hermits—Mokusei Sennin, Kinchi Sennin, and Randatsu Sennin—are said to have appeared.
Izusan Gongen was designated a {{ill|Chokugansho|ja|勅願所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokugansho|1=Chokugansho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q22131160}} (imperial prayer site) during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}}. Subsequent emperors, including {{ill|Emperor Seinei|en|Emperor Seinei|qid=Q357273|lt=Emperor Seinei|1=Emperor Seinei}}, {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|en|Emperor Bidatsu|qid=Q316637|lt=Emperor Bidatsu|1=Emperor Bidatsu}}, {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Emperor Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}, and {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|en|Emperor Kōtoku|qid=Q317980|lt=Emperor Kōtoku|1=Emperor Kōtoku}}, also declared it as such.<ref name="jinja" />
In {{ill|699|en|699|qid=Q23493|lt=699|1=699}} ({{ill|Bunmu 3|ja|文武天皇3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunmu 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579529|1=699}}), {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|qid=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja|1=En no Gyōja}}, the founder of Shugendō, established a hall at Sōtōzan, transforming it into a major training site for ascetic monks. Izusan Sansho Gongen came to be associated with different Buddhist deities: its *hōtai* (法躰) form was the manifestation of Senju Kannon, its *zokutai* (俗躰) form was the manifestation of Amida Nyorai, and its *nyotai* (女躰) form was the manifestation of Nyoirin Kannon.
In {{ill|836|en|836|qid=Q24957|lt=836|1=836}} ({{ill|Jōwa 3|ja|承和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōwa 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579530|1=836}}), the shrine was relocated to its present-day site at Izusan Shrine.<ref name="jinja" />
=== Kamakura and Muromachi Periods === Izusan Gongen, alongside {{ill|Hakone Gongen|ja|箱根権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Gongen|qq=}}, was one of the "Two Shrines" (*Nisho Gongen*). During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, it became the second-most venerated religious site for {{ill|Kantō samurai|ja|関東武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō samurai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, after {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|en|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|qid=Q701403|lt=Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū|1=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū}}. According to the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|qid=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|qid=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}, who had been exiled to {{ill|Izu Province|en|Izu Province|qid=Q860928|lt=Izu Province|1=Izu Province}} following the {{ill|Heiji rebellion|en|Heiji rebellion|qid=Q944859|lt=Heiji Rebellion|1=Heiji rebellion}}, prayed at Izusan Gongen in {{ill|1180|en|1180|qid=Q19702|lt=1180|1=1180}} ({{ill|Jishō 4|ja|治承4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jishō 4|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1180年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1180年|1=1180|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q19702}}) for the restoration of the Genji clan.<ref name="izusan">[https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kanko/kankoshisetsu/1001839/1001864/1001868.html History of Izusan] - Atami City, accessed February 8, 2020.</ref> After establishing the Kamakura Shogunate, Yoritomo made substantial donations to Izusan Gongen, which was recognized as the "General Guardian of the Eight Provinces of Kantō" (*Kan-hasshū no Sōchinju*).<ref name="izusan" />
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, pilgrims visiting the shrine often combined their journey with hot spring treatments in Atami.<ref name="jinja" /> The shrine was also designated as a {{ill|Chokugansho|ja|勅願所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokugansho|1=Chokugansho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q22131160}} by {{ill|Emperor Go-Nara|en|Emperor Go-Nara|qid=Q349467|lt=Emperor Go-Nara|1=Emperor Go-Nara}}, who personally donated a handwritten sutra to the shrine.<ref name="jinja" />
=== Early Modern and Modern Periods === In {{ill|1590|en|1590|qid=Q6764|lt=1590|1=1590}} ({{ill|Tenshō 18|ja|天正18年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō 18|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), during {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s {{ill|Siege of Odawara (1590)|en|Siege of Odawara (1590)|qid=Q2622890|lt=Siege of Odawara|1=Siege of Odawara (1590)}}, Izusan Gongen was set on fire by Hideyoshi’s forces due to its association with the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|qid=Q431928|lt=Late Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}}.{{Sfn|Atami City History Editorial Committee|1967|p=641}}
Between {{ill|1594|en|1594|qid=Q6777|lt=1594|1=1594}} and {{ill|1612|en|1612|qid=Q6771|lt=1612|1=1612}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} appointed the monk Kaiun from {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|qid=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya|1=Mount Kōya}} to restore the site, granting the temple the name *Sōtōzan Hannya-in*. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Izusan became a major religious center, housing 12 temple lodges and 7 Shugendō training halls. Pilgrims, including feudal lords, would frequently visit Izusan Gongen while partaking in Atami's hot springs.<ref name="izusan" />
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} policy led to the abolition of Izusan Gongen. The shrine was divided into {{ill|Izusan Shrine|en|Izusan Shrine|qid=Q611460|lt=Izusan Shrine|1=Izusan Shrine}} and the Shingon-sect temple *Sōtōzan Hannya-in*.
== Surviving Temples == A few temples continue to enshrine Izusan Gongen despite the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|qid=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} movement.
* {{ill|Sōtōzan Hannya-in|ja|般若院 (熱海市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōtōzan Hannya-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Atami, Shizuoka)
{{translated page|ja|伊豆山権現|version=97819890|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:伊豆山権現]]
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11380566}} [[Category:Gongen]] [[Category:Pages linked to by wikidata]] [[Category:Shugendō deities]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
